So I think part of my journey, just to give folks a little bit of context, was I was right after I came back from my fraternity leave, I was just dealing with a lot of stuff around anxiety and things before that, and then when I got back, and there's a lot of career ups and downs, and being a marketing leader is really hard.

We're the first to get fired all the time, and you know, and like, and so like when I got back from fraternity leave, was a CMO at Deepgram, and one of the things I just wanted to do was just get back to things that energize me, right?

And I actually came up with this, let me try to find, I had this slide on kind of, essentially like, what are the things that energize you the most, and what are the things that drain you the most, and and, and so what I started to do was just like, how do I, first of
Find productivity hacks so that I have the time to go do the things that energize me, that I actually want to do, right?
And so I started to cluster my days, and then I hired an EA virtual assistant, essentially, offshore, who's awesome, fairly inexpensive, and just started to do a bunch of things, right?
So this is a little bit of, like, the first lesson I would say in becoming AI native is find the time to become AI native and go super deep without a purpose, even, you know?
And if you don't have the time and you're, quote, unquote, too busy, you're eventually going to have the time because you won't have a job.
So if you find the time while you have a job, then wait until you don't have a job.

And the reason I say this is because there's multiple people in my network that, for whatever reason, layoffs or different reasons, that I'm not having a job or chose to quit, and then went super deep in being an AI native, and now they're, like, kind of reinventing.
How they approach work and how they approach everything in their lives, essentially, right?

But if you don't go through that, let's call it a thousand hours, like, I mean, the first 100 hours I think will already make a huge impact, but if you don't go through like a solid, like, you know, hundreds and hundreds of hours, and I'd say probably a thousand to really feel like you understand it, it's gonna be really hard.
So the first thing you need to figure out is like, where do you find that time?
How do you block that time?
And how do you go deeper?
Because like, a good way to think about it is if you do one hour a week, it's gonna take you two years to get to 100 hours, right?

If you take one day a week, okay, that's good.

But one of the the hacks that I was speaking at Sester last year, and I mentioned this, and I think that was the number one thing that I mentioned that I saw everybody like, kind of writing down and people's eyes kind of opening up very fast was like, take two weeks off, just like, but instead of taking a vacation.
Don't tell your partners or anybody you're doing that, you know, because they'll then ask you to do all your honey-do lists, but take two weeks off to just go, like, super deep.
So I think that was, like, the biggest thing for me that made a difference was, like, carving out those two days a week and going super, super deep.
